Q1.risk_note: January through March is the highest-risk period for {accident_lower} at {airport}. Ice and snow accumulation at outdoor surfaces, tracked moisture into terminals, and reduced visibility at curbside areas all peak during this quarter.

Q2.risk_note: Spring thaw at {airport} creates freeze-thaw cycling that damages outdoor surfaces — uneven pavement, heaved walkways, and drainage issues create a secondary {accident_lower} risk as winter infrastructure damage becomes visible.

Q3.risk_note: Summer at {airport} brings the highest passenger volumes of the year. Peak crowds reduce cleaning frequency in high-traffic areas — a documented pattern that correlates with increased {accident_lower} incidents during July and August.

Q4.risk_note: October through December marks the onset of winter conditions at {airport}. The first major snow and ice events each season — before maintenance protocols are fully operational — historically produce the highest concentration of {accident_lower} incidents.

Q1.risk_note: January through March is peak rain season in {city}. Constant moisture tracking at {airport} entrances means {accident_lower} risk is at its seasonal high — particularly at terminal entries and curbside zones.

Q2.risk_note: Spring at {airport} brings variable weather — dry days interrupted by rain events that wet surfaces quickly. The uneven moisture pattern means wet floor management is more reactive and less systematic during this quarter.

Q3.risk_note: Summer brings the highest passenger volume to {airport}, which strains cleaning crew capacity. The combination of peak crowds and maintenance resources spread thin creates a documented increase in unaddressed floor hazards.

Q4.risk_note: Autumn rains return to {city} from October onward, reactivating the moisture-tracking hazard at {airport} after the relatively dry summer. The first major rain events each fall season historically coincide with spikes in {accident_lower} reports.

Q1.risk_note: Winter at {airport} brings cooler temperatures and occasional precipitation — rare but significant because desert airports are not equipped for wet conditions the way northern airports are. Even minor rain creates disproportionate hazards.

Q2.risk_note: Spring in {city} brings wind-borne dust that accumulates on terminal floors at {airport} — creating a less visible but documented slip hazard when dust mixes with foot traffic moisture.

Q3.risk_label: Peak summer heat — highest risk quarter
Q3.risk_note: Summer is the highest-risk quarter at {airport}. Extreme heat causes passenger dehydration and disorientation. Condensation from air conditioning creates moisture near entry points. Combined with peak passenger volume, summer produces the highest {accident_lower} incident rates.

Q4.risk_note: Late-season monsoon weather in {city} creates brief but intense rain events at {airport} that maintenance protocols are not designed to handle. The October-November transition period combines residual heat with unexpected moisture events.

Q1.risk_note: Winter in the South brings occasional ice storms that are particularly hazardous at airports like {airport} because southern facilities are equipped for minimal winter weather. A single ice event can overwhelm maintenance capacity entirely.

Q2.risk_note: Spring thunderstorm season in {city} creates frequent sudden wet conditions at {airport}. Rapid-onset storms produce tracked moisture before maintenance crews can respond, creating a concentrated {accident_lower} risk during each storm event.

Q3.risk_label: Peak summer heat and humidity — highest risk quarter
Q3.risk_note: Summer is the highest-risk quarter at {airport}. High humidity means floor surfaces never fully dry between cleaning cycles. Peak passenger volume during summer travel season combines with the highest annual moisture levels to produce the most {accident_lower} claims per quarter.

Q4.risk_label: Hurricane season tail and fall transition
Q4.risk_note: October through December sees the tail end of hurricane season and the transition to winter in {city}. Post-tropical weather events at {airport} can create extended periods of wet conditions that persist long after the storm passes.

Q1.risk_note: January through March represents peak winter hazard at {airport}. Deep snowpack, extreme cold, and frozen surfaces at all outdoor zones create severe {accident_lower} conditions that are among the most hazardous of any US airport during this quarter.

Q2.risk_note: Spring in {city} brings freeze-thaw cycling and melt conditions that create saturated outdoor surfaces and tracked mud into terminals at {airport}. The spring melt period is historically one of the higher-incident quarters for {accident_lower} claims.

Q3.risk_label: Summer peak travel and afternoon storms
Q3.risk_note: Summer brings peak tourist traffic to {airport} along with afternoon thunderstorms common to mountain climates. The combination of maximum passenger volume and daily afternoon storm events produces a sustained elevated {accident_lower} risk throughout July and August.

Q4.risk_label: First snow events — transition hazard period
Q4.risk_note: October through December marks the first snow events of the season at {airport}. The transition from summer maintenance protocols to winter operations creates a gap period when the first ice and snow events arrive before the facility is fully prepared.

Q1.risk_note: Winter and early spring bring ice, rain, and tracked moisture to {airport}. The risk is moderate but consistent — particularly at terminal entries, curbside, and parking zones during precipitation events.

Q2.risk_note: Spring weather variation at {airport} creates alternating wet and dry surface conditions that challenge maintenance protocols. Uneven response to changing weather is a documented pattern that creates {accident_lower} hazard windows.

Q3.risk_note: Summer brings maximum passenger volume to {airport}, straining cleaning crew capacity in high-traffic zones. July and August historically show the highest absolute number of {accident_lower} incidents at airports of this size.

Q4.risk_label: Fall and early winter hazard onset
Q4.risk_note: Autumn at {airport} marks the return of precipitation and the onset of winter conditions. The first frost events and early rain season create a concentrated {accident_lower} hazard period before winter maintenance protocols are fully activated.

Q1.risk_note: The dry season in {city} reduces but does not eliminate {accident_lower} risk at {airport}. Year-round humidity means floors never reach truly dry conditions, and the lower rain frequency does not significantly reduce the maintenance obligation.

Q2.risk_note: Late spring in {city} brings increasing humidity and the onset of the rainy season at {airport}. May and June historically see rising {accident_lower} incident rates as moisture management at the facility becomes more demanding.

Q3.risk_label: Peak hurricane season — highest risk quarter
Q3.risk_note: Summer is the highest-risk quarter at {airport}. Peak hurricane season combines extreme precipitation events with the year's highest passenger volume. Post-tropical weather events create extended wet conditions that overwhelm standard floor maintenance protocols.

Q4.risk_note: October through November remains in the tail of hurricane season in {city}. Late-season tropical weather events can create surprise high-moisture periods at {airport} that maintenance crews are not expecting given the apparent transition to drier conditions.
